plan.thesis: Prompt caching lets you pay once to store a large context and reuse it at a fraction of the cost — but only when applied to the right workloads.
plan.targetTakeaway: Readers will leave knowing exactly which of their LLM calls are cacheable, how to configure caching in the Anthropic API, and what 60–90% cost reductions look like in practice.

sections.title: Why Your LLM Costs Are Higher Than They Need to Be
sections.intent: Hook the reader with a concrete cost-pain scenario; introduce caching as the solution.
keyPoints: A typical RAG call re-sends the same 10k-token system prompt every time
That's ~$0.03 per call multiplied by millions of requests
Prompt caching changes the economics fundamentally

sections.title: How Prompt Caching Works: Tokens, TTL, and Cache Keys
sections.intent: Explain the technical mechanism: what gets cached, for how long, and how the provider identifies cache hits.
keyPoints: Cache is keyed on the exact prefix of the messages array
Anthropic's TTL is 5 minutes (extendable via usage)
Input tokens on a cache hit cost ~10% of full price
cache_control breakpoint markers in the API

profile.name: Habr
profile.audience: Russian-speaking backend engineers with 3–10 years of experience

slot.brief: An overhead editorial photo of an open laptop on a wooden desk, a stylized translucent overlay showing duplicated lines of code being collapsed into a single cached block, conveying the idea of paying once and reusing many times.

snapshot.subject: An open laptop on a clean wooden desk, viewed from a slight overhead angle. A semi-transparent overlay shows duplicated lines of code being collapsed into a single highlighted cached block above the keyboard.
snapshot.style: editorial photo with subtle digital overlay
snapshot.composition: centered, slight overhead three-quarter angle, shallow depth of field with the laptop screen sharp and the desk surface softened
snapshot.lighting: soft window light from the upper left, warm ambient fill
snapshot.negative: no logos, no readable brand names, no human faces
